response:
Call update_projects with next_review_date set directly.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-abc", "next_review_date": "2026-04-15"}])

The next_review_date parameter overrides the calculated review schedule. No need to change last_reviewed or review_interval.

Call update_projects to enable completed_by_children.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-xyz", "completed_by_children": true}])

This makes the project auto-complete when its last remaining task is completed.

No tool call needed beyond reading existing data. Call get_tasks with the task's ID and check the nextDueDate field.

get_tasks(task_id="<task_id_of_weekly_standup>")

The response includes nextDueDate (and nextDeferDate, nextPlannedDate) for recurring tasks, populated without needing to complete the current occurrence.

Use get_projects with the stalled_only filter.

get_projects(stalled_only=true)

This returns active projects with no available next actions — exactly the stuck/needs-attention projects.

No tool call needed — this is an explanation question. Check the catchUpAutomatically field on the task.

get_tasks(task_id="<task_id_of_take_vitamins>")

Look at catchUpAutomatically in the response:
- If true: you'll get ONE catch-up occurrence when you complete it (not 5 separate ones).
- If false: each missed interval spawns its own occurrence, so you could see multiple overdue tasks.

The repetitionMethod also matters — 'due_after_completion' means the next due date is calculated from when you actually complete, so missed days don't pile up.

No tool call needed — this is a warning/explanation. If the 'Priority' parent tag has childrenAreMutuallyExclusive set to true, then adding 'Low' via update_tasks(add_tags=['Low']) will SILENTLY remove 'High'. The task will end up with only 'Low', not both.

To check, call get_tags and look at the Priority parent tag's childrenAreMutuallyExclusive field. If it's true, assigning one child automatically removes its siblings.

Call create_projects with due_date and defer_date.

create_projects([{"name": "Q3 Report", "folder_path": "Work", "due_date": "2026-07-15", "defer_date": "2026-06-01"}])

The defer_date hides the project until June 1, and due_date sets the July 15 deadline.

Call update_projects with due_date set to empty string to clear it.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-abc", "due_date": ""}])

Call get_projects with the project_id to retrieve its date fields.

get_projects(project_id="proj-xyz")

The response includes dueDate, deferDate, and plannedDate fields.

Call update_tasks to reparent the task under the new parent.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-101", "parent_task_id": "task-200"}])

This moves 'Write introduction' from the inbox to become a subtask of 'Draft report'. The parent_task_id parameter handles reparenting.

Call get_tasks with a query search.

get_tasks(query="mortgage")

This searches task names and notes for 'mortgage' (case-insensitive) across all projects.

Call get_tags to retrieve all tags and check the parentTagId field.

get_tags()

In the results, find the tag named 'High' and check its parentTagId. Then find the tag whose id matches that parentTagId to confirm it's 'Energy'.

Call update_tasks with status='dropped' to abandon the task without deleting it.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-500", "status": "dropped"}])

This marks the task as dropped (abandoned) while preserving it in the database.

Call update_projects to drop all three projects in a single batch call.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-A", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-B", "status": "dropped"}, {"id": "proj-C", "status": "dropped"}])

No tool call needed — this is an explanation. Tasks in a completed project have completed=false (their own state) but available=false (inherited from the completed container). The server instructions state: 'completed/dropped reflect the task's own state, not its container's. Task in completed project: completed: false, available: false.'

If you're seeing these tasks, you're likely not filtering with available_only=True. Use get_tasks(available_only=true) to exclude tasks that are in completed/dropped projects.

Call reorder_project to move proj-C before proj-A.

reorder_project(project_id="proj-C", before_project_id="proj-A")

Two calls needed. First create the parent tag with mutual exclusivity, then create the child tags.

1. create_tags([{"name": "Priority", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}])

2. create_tags([{"name": "High", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Medium", "parent_tag": "Priority"}, {"name": "Low", "parent_tag": "Priority"}])

The children_are_mutually_exclusive flag on the parent ensures assigning one child tag silently removes its siblings.

Call update_tags to enable mutual exclusivity on the existing parent tag.

update_tags([{"id": "tag-energy-001", "children_are_mutually_exclusive": true}])

This changes the Energy tag so that only one child (High/Medium/Low Energy) can be assigned to a task at a time. Existing tasks with multiple energy tags won't be retroactively cleaned up, but future assignments will enforce exclusivity.

Call update_projects to flag and tag the project in one call.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-website-001", "flagged": true, "add_tags": ["High Priority"]}])

Call reorder_task to move QA testing before Frontend build.

reorder_task(task_id="task-qa-001", before_task_id="task-fe-001")

Call delete_tags to permanently remove the tag.

delete_tags(tag_ids="tag-errands-001")

This permanently deletes the tag. Tasks that had this tag will lose the association but are not themselves deleted. This is different from dropping (update_tags with status='dropped'), which just hides the tag.

Call get_perspectives to list all perspectives.

get_perspectives()

The response includes name, type (built-in or custom), and id. Filter the results for type='custom' to see only custom perspectives.

Call create_folders with parent_path.

create_folders([{"name": "Q2", "parent_path": "Work"}])

Call switch_perspective.

switch_perspective(perspective_name="Weekly Review")

Two-step process. First get overdue tasks, then flag the most urgent.

1. get_tasks(overdue=true)

2. After reviewing results, identify the 3 most urgent (by due date or judgment), then:

update_tasks([{"id": "<task1_id>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<task2_id>", "flagged": true}, {"id": "<task3_id>", "flagged": true}])

First search for the project, then get its tasks if found.

1. get_projects(query="Quantum Computing Research")

2. If a project is found, use its ID:
   get_tasks(project_id="<found_project_id>")

If no project is found, inform the user that the project doesn't exist.

Call create_tasks with tags as a list. No project_id means inbox.

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Buy birthday gift", "tags": ["Computer", "Errands"]}])

Call create_tasks with all 5 tasks in a single batch. No project_id means inbox.

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Buy groceries"}, {"task_name": "Do laundry"}, {"task_name": "Wash dishes"}, {"task_name": "Vacuum living room"}, {"task_name": "Prep meals for the week"}])

Call create_projects with both projects in a single batch.

create_projects([{"name": "Kitchen Renovation", "project_type": "sequential", "folder_path": "Home"}, {"name": "Garden Planning", "project_type": "parallel", "folder_path": "Home"}])

Create the parent task as a sequential action group, then add subtasks in order.

1. create_tasks([{"task_name": "Event Planning", "project_id": "proj-100", "sequential": true}])

2. After getting the parent task ID from the response:

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Research venues", "parent_task_id": "<event_planning_task_id>"}, {"task_name": "Book venue", "parent_task_id": "<event_planning_task_id>"}, {"task_name": "Send invitations", "parent_task_id": "<event_planning_task_id>"}])

Since the parent is sequential, the subtasks will be completed in order — 'Book venue' is blocked until 'Research venues' is done, etc.

Create a parallel project, then use sequential action groups to model the two dependency chains.

1. create_projects([{"name": "Home Renovation", "project_type": "parallel"}])

2. Using the project ID, create two sequential action groups:

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Contractor Chain", "project_id": "<proj_id>", "sequential": true}, {"task_name": "Paint Chain", "project_id": "<proj_id>", "sequential": true}])

3. Add subtasks to each chain:

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Get contractor quotes", "parent_task_id": "<contractor_chain_id>"}, {"task_name": "Schedule contractor", "parent_task_id": "<contractor_chain_id>"}, {"task_name": "Choose paint colors", "parent_task_id": "<paint_chain_id>"}, {"task_name": "Buy paint", "parent_task_id": "<paint_chain_id>"}])

The parallel project lets both chains proceed independently. Within each sequential action group, tasks must be done in order.

Three steps to build the nested structure.

1. Create the top-level sequential action group:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Prepare presentation", "project_id": "proj-200", "sequential": true}])

2. Add sequential subtasks using the parent task ID:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Research topic", "parent_task_id": "<prepare_pres_id>"}, {"task_name": "Write outline", "parent_task_id": "<prepare_pres_id>"}, {"task_name": "Create slides", "parent_task_id": "<prepare_pres_id>"}])

3. Under 'Create slides', add parallel subtasks (set sequential=false on 'Create slides' if needed, but parallel is the default for action groups — actually, the parent inherits the project's sequential setting, so we need to explicitly set it):

First, update 'Create slides' to be parallel:
update_tasks([{"id": "<create_slides_id>", "sequential": false}])

Then add its subtasks:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Design template", "parent_task_id": "<create_slides_id>"}, {"task_name": "Add content", "parent_task_id": "<create_slides_id>"}, {"task_name": "Add animations", "parent_task_id": "<create_slides_id>"}])

OmniFocus doesn't support true diamond dependencies (task blocked by multiple independent predecessors). The closest approximation uses a sequential action group.

Option: Create a sequential action group with a parallel sub-group for the two independent tasks, then 'Review findings' after.

1. create_tasks([{"task_name": "Report Work", "project_id": "proj-300", "sequential": true}])

2. Create a parallel sub-group for the independent tasks:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Research Phase", "parent_task_id": "<report_work_id>", "sequential": false}])

Then add 'Review findings' as a sibling after the sub-group:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Draft report", "parent_task_id": "<research_phase_id>"}, {"task_name": "Collect data", "parent_task_id": "<research_phase_id>"}, {"task_name": "Review findings", "parent_task_id": "<report_work_id>"}])

Since 'Report Work' is sequential, 'Review findings' won't become available until all tasks in the 'Research Phase' action group are complete. This gives the diamond dependency effect: both 'Draft report' and 'Collect data' can be done in parallel, but 'Review findings' waits for both.

Per the server instructions: 'Dropping a recurring task without clearing recurrence first spawns the next occurrence.' To stop the entire series, clear recurrence AND drop in a single update.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-weekly-123", "recurrence": "", "status": "dropped"}])

Both fields must be set in the same call to avoid spawning another occurrence.

Call update_tags to move the tag under a new parent by name.

update_tags([{"id": "tag-aimee-001", "parent_tag": "People"}])

The parent_tag parameter accepts a tag name (not ID). Existing task associations are preserved — only the tag's position in the hierarchy changes.
